EventType.comment: Examples: ELECTION_OUTCOME, MACROECONOMIC_THRESHOLD, REGULATORY_DECISION, CLIMATE_POLICY_OUTCOME, GEOPOLITICAL_OUTCOME

EventDescription.source_validation: Must be anchored to the same OfficialResolutionSource used to resolve the event outcome.
EventDescription.comment: Plain-language event description. Length and character-set limits support repository search, deduplication, and manipulation review.

EventJurisdiction.comment: Jurisdiction whose institutions / decisions resolve the event. Distinct from PlatformJurisdiction.

EventOfficialSource.comment: Authoritative source whose release resolves the contract: BLS, Eurostat, ESMA, national election authority, IPCC, etc.

EventObservationDate.comment: Date on which the event is observed and the contract is resolvable. Distinct from settlement date.

PayoutAmount.comment: Per-contract payout if event resolves positive.

SettlementCurrency.comment: Extends ISO 4217 with an approved-token codeset to capture USDC and similar stablecoin settlement legs.

DeliveryType.comment: Binary event contracts settle through cash or token cash-equivalent payout rather than physical delivery.

PlatformType.comment: Captures venue-choice dimension that drives regulatory arbitrage. Distinct from EventJurisdiction.

HedgingPurposeDeclared.comment: Self-declared by reporting counterparty. Engine does not verify. Mis-declaration carries the same legal weight as other self-declared regulatory fields (e.g., EMIR clearing threshold flag).

DesignNotes: 1. The schema describes the product, not the regulatory obligation. Whether an EventContract triggers an EMIR or CFTC reporting duty is a separate legal determination.

2. The schema treats event-based instruments as a first-class asset class, not as a subtype of the DSB 'Other' / Non-Standard template. The underlier of an event contract is not a price series; forcing it into 'Other' loses analytical resolution.

3. PlatformType is a structural field, not a derived one. The same economic exposure on a CFTC DCM (T026, T028) vs an offshore decentralised platform (T027) has materially different regulatory implications and the schema must capture this distinction at the UPI layer.

4. HedgingPurposeDeclared is self-declared. The engine cannot verify hedging intent; the schema documents what the reporter claims, leaves verification to supervisory review, and creates an auditable record.

5. SettlementCurrency permits an approved-token codeset alongside ISO 4217. The current ISO codeset cannot describe USDC, USDT, or other stablecoin settlement legs used by decentralised event-contract venues; without an extension, every such trade fails currency validation even when the contract is otherwise reportable.

6. EventJurisdiction and PlatformJurisdiction (via PlatformType) are deliberately separated. A German firm hedging a German election outcome on a US-regulated venue must be distinguishable at the UPI level from the same firm hedging the same outcome on an EU-licensed gaming operator.

ConsistencyWithDsbPrinciples.ISO_4914_compliance: Same hierarchical AssetClass/InstrumentType/UseCase structure as existing DSB templates.
ConsistencyWithDsbPrinciples.Codeset_reuse: Reuses ISOCurrencyCode, ISO3166Country where possible; only introduces new codesets (EventTypeCode) where existing ones cannot describe the product.
ConsistencyWithDsbPrinciples.Multi_asset_support: Does not require multi-asset extensions; event contracts are single-event-underlier instruments by construction.
ConsistencyWithDsbPrinciples.OTC_ISIN_parent_child_relationship: Each EventContract UPI may have many OTC ISIN children (one per specific event resolution window), consistent with the DSB UPI -> OTC ISIN hierarchy.
